title: In God We Trust

jokes.text: Michael Bluth was fed up with his mother's extravagant spending. / 'Like it was yesterday.' / 'It was this morning.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Mom remembers the conversation about cutting back, expecting a 'yes'
jokes.punchline: She says 'Like it was yesterday' — it was literally that morning, exposing her total indifference
jokes.explanation: Perfectly efficient cold-open joke that establishes Lucille's character in two lines. The misdirection ('Like it was yesterday') followed by the literal correction ('It was this morning') is airtight. High character integration — this couldn't work without Lucille's specific brand of oblivious selfishness.

jokes.text: To throw the most lavish party this town has ever seen for my birthday.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to appeal to frugality; Lucille says she has a reason for the yacht crew
jokes.punchline: The reason is the exact opposite of frugality — the most lavish party possible
jokes.explanation: Functional but expected beat — Lucille's counter-request being maximally extravagant is in-character but not surprising. The delivery sells it more than the writing.

jokes.text: I enjoy bicentennials as much as the next guy, Mom, but we're not doing that.
jokes.setup: Mom wants to throw herself the most lavish party this town has ever seen
jokes.punchline: Michael concedes he enjoys bicentennials — implying her party ambitions are on the scale of a national celebration — then shoots it down flatly
jokes.explanation: Sharp and unexpected. Michael's deadpan concession that he 'enjoys bicentennials' is a great comic non sequitur that still logically lands — it frames Mom's party as a once-per-200-years event. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: Narrator describes Buster accidentally flirting, accidentally bidding $10,000 at a charity auction, and accidentally entering her apartment saying 'I need you right now. Fellas, take a powder.'
jokes.setup: Narrator explains Buster had been avoiding Lucille Austero
jokes.punchline: Three escalating 'accidents' — each more compromising than the last, culminating in him apparently walking in on her with male company and dismissing them
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best sequences. The rule-of-three structure with 'accidentally' repeated is perfectly calibrated — the absurdity escalates beautifully each time. The 'Fellas, take a powder' image is the peak: we see Buster, who can barely function around women, somehow walking into a scene and imperiously dismissing her companions. Pure Arrested Development layered comedy. The structure alone earns an 8+.

jokes.text: Oh, here comes the 10:15 conniption, right on time. / Honest to God, Buster, it's like every little thing makes you seize up in terror lately. I have no idea where you get that from. Get away from that stove. You're going to light your hair on fire.
jokes.setup: Lucille says she has no idea where Buster's anxiety comes from
jokes.punchline: In the same breath, she demonstrates exactly where it comes from by calling his panic by a scheduled time and immediately issuing a terrifying warning
jokes.explanation: An exceptional piece of character comedy. The joke is entirely in the irony: Lucille genuinely has no self-awareness that she is the cause of Buster's terror, demonstrated simultaneously by (a) having a name and schedule for his panic attacks and (b) immediately threatening him. 'I have no idea where you get that from' followed instantly by a hysterical safety warning is the kind of self-undercutting irony that defines this show's best writing. Highly quotable and deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: He's weak. Speaking of weak, Mom, what do you got in there, gold bars?
jokes.setup: Michael makes an offhand joke about the heavy bag being full of gold bars
jokes.punchline: Paid off later — the bag literally contains gold Krugerrands
jokes.explanation: On first watch, a throwaway one-liner. On rewatch, it's a precision-planted seed — Michael's joke-guess is literally correct. This is a hallmark of AD's layered construction. The joke itself is mild on first viewing (hence 6.5 impact) but the rewatch bonus is significant. The craft score reflects how elegantly it's embedded.

jokes.text: Protein bars for your father. He's pumping up in prison.
jokes.setup: Michael asks what's in the heavy bag
jokes.punchline: It's 'protein bars' for dad who is 'pumping up in prison' — casual mention of prison fitness as if totally normal
jokes.explanation: A decent throwaway with rewatch value — the protein bars are gold Krugerrands, not protein bars at all, making this a planted lie that reads as casual comedy. On first viewing it's mildly funny; on rewatch it's a delicious deception.

jokes.text: No more parties. Okay, Mom? / You're my third least-favorite child.
jokes.setup: Michael asserts parental authority and asks Mom to agree
jokes.punchline: Lucille retaliates with a ranked insult that implies she has a whole hierarchy of least-favorite children
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable and character-perfect. The joke is in the specificity: not 'least favorite' but 'third least-favorite,' implying a meticulous ranking system Lucille maintains. Michael's immediate acceptance ('I can live with that') is the perfect button. This is Lucille at her most economically devastating.

jokes.text: I can live with that.
jokes.setup: Lucille informs Michael he's her third least-favorite child
jokes.punchline: Michael's total calm acceptance — he doesn't even dispute the ranking, just accepts it with quiet resignation
jokes.explanation: The button to joke #8 — four words that land perfectly. Michael's acceptance of his 'third least-favorite' status is the punchline's punchline. Economy score is near-perfect: nothing could be cut.

jokes.text: Michael came home to find his mother's fourth least-favorite child.
jokes.setup: Just established that Michael is the third least-favorite child
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately uses this newly established hierarchy to introduce Gob as 'fourth least-favorite'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's cleverest structural moves. The joke from 20 seconds ago is immediately weaponized by the narrator to introduce the next character, validating the hierarchy as a real system within the show's world. The instantaneous callback is perfectly executed — it rewards attentive viewers and adds a new layer of absurdity to the ranking joke.

jokes.text: Well, they got the Asian right. 'Hotties' might be a stretch.
jokes.setup: Gob is referring to some promotional material or listing for the yacht (apparently labeled 'Asian Hotties' or similar)
jokes.punchline: He concedes one accurate descriptor while casually dismissing the other — delivered with complete Gob self-assurance
jokes.explanation: A Gob-specific joke that works on his obliviousness but relies somewhat on mild shock value. The structure is fine but it's not among the episode's stronger moments.

jokes.text: Well, let's start with the little one.
jokes.setup: Gob has just critiqued the 'hotties' label for the prospective yacht buyers
jokes.punchline: He suggests starting negotiations with the smaller/less impressive prospect, playing the situation like a real estate or dating scenario
jokes.explanation: A mild Gob moment — functional character comedy but not particularly inventive.

jokes.text: I'm sure there'd be somebody else out there that would appreciate her. In fact, Michael felt that man was himself.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests Gob break up with Marta because someone else would appreciate her
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Michael himself is that someone — he's selflessly advising his brother to dump the woman he's secretly infatuated with
jokes.explanation: Great use of the narrator as ironic commentator — the reveal flips Michael's 'generous' advice into self-serving hypocrisy he's not even fully aware of. The structure (character says X, narrator explains the real meaning) is an AD specialty executed cleanly here.

jokes.text: I wanna throw Mom a party. / Would you... I just told Mom no more parties. / I know, Michael. That's why this is the perfect opportunity to throw her a surprise party. She'd never suspect it.
jokes.setup: Michael explicitly told Mom no more parties; Gob immediately proposes one
jokes.punchline: Gob's logic: telling Mom no parties means she won't expect a surprise party — treating Michael's 'no' as a strategic setup rather than an actual refusal
jokes.explanation: Solid Gob logic — his reasoning is internally consistent in the way only a self-deluded person's can be. 'She'd never suspect it' as a justification for doing exactly what Michael prohibited is a good character beat.

jokes.text: Michael, do you have any idea what would happen if I were to visit prison? / Hmm. / It would be pandemonium. / Mm-hmm.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests Lindsay visit their father in jail; she resists dramatically
jokes.punchline: Lindsay says it would be 'pandemonium' — implying prison inmates would riot over her beauty; Michael's deadpan 'Mm-hmm' is a masterclass in understated skepticism
jokes.explanation: Beautifully compressed. Lindsay's delusion is established in one word ('pandemonium') and Michael's reaction is two syllables of gentle disbelief. The gap between Lindsay's self-image and reality is the engine of this entire subplot, crystallized here.

jokes.text: It'd just give Dad one more reason to think that I've got nothing to offer but my looks. / Yeah, I got some of that... except he also didn't like my looks.
jokes.setup: Lindsay complains that her father only values her looks
jokes.punchline: Michael one-ups her: at least Dad acknowledged her looks — Michael didn't even get that
jokes.explanation: Tight and funny. Michael's self-deprecating one-liner lands because it pivots from sympathizing with Lindsay to topping her complaint. 'Except he also didn't like my looks' is economically devastating — it's a stealth punchline that reveals Michael's own wound while undercutting Lindsay's vanity.

jokes.text: Lucille had recently been featured on Fox's World's Worst Drivers.
jokes.setup: Lindsay says Lucille is the world's worst driver (hyperbole it seems)
jokes.punchline: The narrator confirms it literally — she appeared on a Fox show about it
jokes.explanation: Classic AD move: take a hyperbolic character claim and literalize it. The Fox reference adds a meta layer (AD airs on Fox) that sharp viewers catch. The payoff of 'world's worst' being a real designation sets up the entire car accident plot. Good structural work.

jokes.text: She didn't. I dummied her up a new one. Not my best work though. She wanted to look 48. I nearly airbrushed her into oblivion. We ended up checking 'Albino' on the form.
jokes.setup: Michael can't believe Lucille's license was renewed given her driving record
jokes.punchline: Gob reveals he forged her license — then escalates: she wanted to look younger, he nearly erased her ethnicity entirely, and they had to classify her as Albino
jokes.explanation: A superb escalating joke. Each sentence adds a new absurdity: forged license → vanity request → over-airbrushing → checking 'Albino' on the form. The final image is perfect: Lucille's vain attempt to look younger resulting in a legal document declaring her a different race. 'Not my best work' as understatement is excellent character voice for Gob. Highly quotable and visually vivid.

jokes.text: She was having trouble even lifting up this bag of groceries. / All right. We will do the surprise party. / For Gangy? / Yeah, how did you know? / She called and invited us.
jokes.setup: Gob's frail-Lucille act convinces Michael to throw the party; asks how George Michael knew
jokes.punchline: George Michael reveals Lucille called and invited them herself — the 'surprise' party was her idea all along
jokes.explanation: Perfect structural punchline to Gob's 'frail Lucille' manipulation — and it comes from the most innocent character, George Michael, who has no idea he's undercutting the whole scheme. The 'she called and invited us' reveal collapses three characters' scheming instantly. Good AD architecture.

jokes.text: Come on. This is a Bluth family celebration. It's no place for children.
jokes.setup: Children ask if they have to attend the family party
jokes.punchline: Michael excludes children from a family event — the logic inverts the expected parental response
jokes.explanation: Sharp and quotable. The joke inverts the usual parental logic — a family celebration being declared 'no place for children' acknowledges how dysfunctional the Bluth family actually is. Economy is near-perfect; every word earns its place.

jokes.text: Lindsay went to the prison to visit her father for the first time. But didn't get the reception she was expecting.
jokes.setup: Narrator teases that Lindsay visiting prison will be eventful ('didn't get the reception she was expecting')
jokes.punchline: Visual beat: the camera cuts to Lindsay in prison and NO ONE reacts to her — the 'pandemonium' she predicted completely fails to materialize
jokes.explanation: The payoff to Lindsay's 'pandemonium' joke is delivered entirely visually (inferred). The narrator's tease creates expectation; the reality is silence. This callback structure is a perfect example of AD's setup-then-deflate technique. The gap between Lindsay's self-image and the indifferent prisoners is the entire joke — and it's executed without a single spoken punchline.

jokes.text: My whole life, all you've ever praised me for is my looks. / Oh, no. Your looks... / Yes, Dad. It's always been Michael's got the brains, Gob's got the charm, Buster's got the... / High-fastening pants. / Have you said that? / No. I'm saying that now.
jokes.setup: Lindsay confronts George Sr. about how he's categorized his children; she expects him to name Buster's defining trait
jokes.punchline: George Sr. offers 'high-fastening pants' — not a quality, just an observation about Buster's wardrobe — and then admits he's making it up on the spot
jokes.explanation: Excellent. The joke has two layers: (1) George Sr.'s only descriptor for Buster is his fashion choice rather than any personality trait, and (2) he freely admits he just made it up right now. The combination reveals how little he's thought about Buster as a person while being perfectly funny. 'High-fastening pants' as an identifying characteristic is inspired specificity — it's visual, undignified, and completely accurate to Buster's character.

jokes.text: Lindsay was surprised once again that no heads were turned.
jokes.setup: Lindsay dramatically reveals her complaint to George Sr.; 'And a gorgeous head of hair' is George Sr.'s oblivious response praising only her looks again
jokes.punchline: The narrator notes 'once again' no heads were turned — suggesting this has happened before AND that her scene with Dad has replicated her prison entry failure
jokes.explanation: 'Once again' is the key word — it ties this moment to the earlier prison entrance beat and implies this is a pattern in Lindsay's life. The economy of the narrator's line is superb.

jokes.text: I'm gonna go shopping. I'll be back tomorrow.
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just emotionally confronted her father about lifelong neglect
jokes.punchline: Her response to the unresolved emotional conversation is to... go shopping and not come back today
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lindsay character moment — she punctures her own emotional scene with casual consumerism. The brevity ('I'll be back tomorrow') shows her attention span for emotional reckoning is under 90 seconds.

jokes.text: George Michael was finally moving beyond his crush on Maeby and felt happy to have a cousin he could just see a movie with. It was a love between two cousins that the world thought was wrong. But it was the world that was wrong.
jokes.setup: Narrator frames George Michael and Maeby's outing as a pure, innocent cousin activity
jokes.punchline: The narrator then declares it 'a love' and defends it against the world's judgment — a deliberate inversion that the show knows is deeply inappropriate but plays straight
jokes.explanation: One of the show's signature moves: the narrator defending the romantic subplot that everyone knows is wrong. 'It was the world that was wrong' delivered with total earnestness is exactly the kind of subversive dark comedy AD excels at. The setup that George Michael is 'moving beyond his crush' being immediately undercut by declaring it a 'love' is a great fake-out.

jokes.text: We have got to see this movie. / Excuse me, kids? Can I see your I.D.'s?
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby are excited to see a movie called 'Les Cousins Dangereux'
jokes.punchline: They're carded — the film about dangerous/romantic cousins is apparently adult-rated, ironically appropriate given their own situation
jokes.explanation: Dense joke: 'Les Cousins Dangereux' (The Dangerous Cousins) is the film, and they're too young to get in — which mirrors their real situation exactly. The movie title commenting on their relationship, combined with their being barred, is a beautifully layered bit. The rewatch bonus is high because the title becomes richer over the series.

jokes.text: Buster had recently brought the rock home from an archeological dig, but had yet to find a way to get it out of the car.
jokes.setup: Lucille complains about 'Buster's stupid rock' behind her seat
jokes.punchline: The narrator explains Buster has a whole-ass boulder in the car that he simply hasn't figured out how to remove yet
jokes.explanation: The rock becomes plot-critical later (it's what hit Michael on the head), making this a planted detail that reads as gentle Buster absurdism on first viewing. The payoff elevates both jokes retroactively.

jokes.text: Mom, you've already got two strikes on your record. You strike one more person, and it's technically a spree.
jokes.setup: Michael is discouraging his mother from driving; she wants to take the wheel
jokes.punchline: He calculates that hitting one more person with the car would technically make her a 'spree' driver — treating vehicular strikes like a baseball/criminal statistic
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The wordplay on 'strike' (baseball/driving) and 'spree' (crime spree applied to hitting pedestrians) is sharp and original. Michael's clinical accounting of his mother's vehicular violence — like a baseball announcer tracking stats — is perfectly deadpan. Highly quotable, layered, economical. Comparable in craft to the 8.0 reference anchor, arguably exceeding it in originality and quotability.

jokes.text: Surprise! / And she was surprised... that her family didn't show up.
jokes.setup: Michael surprises Lucille at the restaurant — everything seems to be going to plan
jokes.punchline: The family isn't there; she's 'surprised' but in the entirely wrong direction — the surprise party is itself a surprise, but for the wrong reason
jokes.explanation: The narrator's double use of 'surprised' is elegant — the word does two jobs simultaneously (she was shocked by the party / she was shocked by the abandonment). Good structural writing and a satisfying payoff to the party setup.

jokes.text: It's the first I've heard about it. / What about you? Neiman's? / Prison. Then Neiman's. But only because I'm going back to prison, and they've seen me in this.
jokes.setup: Michael confronts the family about missing the party; Lindsay is asked where she was
jokes.punchline: Lindsay was at prison — then Neiman's — and her justification for the shopping is that she can't be seen in the same outfit twice at the prison she's returning to
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character joke. Lindsay went to prison AND Neiman's — but the reason for Neiman's is that she cares about her prison outfit. The implication that prisoners and guards are her fashion audience is absurd and perfectly Lindsay. The economy is impressive: the entire joke is built in one sentence. High quotability.

jokes.text: You barely even leave Mom's apartment. In fact, Buster was worried about running into Lucille 2. So he never left his mother's apartment. Lucille 2 was also supposed to go, but was waiting until she saw Buster emerge.
jokes.setup: Michael asks why Buster didn't come to the party
jokes.punchline: Buster didn't leave because he feared running into Lucille 2; Lucille 2 didn't leave because she was waiting for Buster — a perfect Mexican standoff of mutual avoidance
jokes.explanation: Structurally elegant: the two characters are locked in a perfect comedic stalemate — each waiting for the other to move first. The irony that their mutual avoidance caused the same outcome (both staying put) is efficiently conveyed by the narrator in a few sentences. Classic AD information-compression.

jokes.text: I mean, it would've been funny if it wasn't so... Well, it was kind of funny.
jokes.setup: Michael describes Lucille going from 'giddy to devastated' at the empty party
jokes.punchline: He catches himself starting to say it wasn't funny, then immediately admits it was
jokes.explanation: A quick self-aware beat — Michael's honesty about finding his mother's devastation funny is good character work, and the unfinished sentence pivot is clean. Brief but effective.

jokes.text: What frail act got you this time, Michael? She couldn't open a jar? What, did a clump of her hair come out in your hands?
jokes.setup: Michael is being guilted by Mom's apparent frailty; Gob calls out the pattern
jokes.punchline: Gob enumerates specific 'frail acts' — jar-opening failure and literal hair loss — as Lucille's manipulation tactics, showing he's seen this before
jokes.explanation: Gob's enumerated 'frailty plays' are funny because they're specific (the jar, the hair clump) and because Gob — not exactly the most perceptive person — is the one who has clocked Mom's manipulation. The visual specificity of 'hair come out in your hands' is particularly good.

jokes.text: You know, Mom, I'm sorry you're upset. But if you were nicer, then maybe your kids would take better care of you... or wanna be with you more.
jokes.setup: Lucille is upset at dinner; Michael tries to comfort her but pivots to honesty
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'comfort' becomes a gentle indictment — 'if you were nicer' is devastating parental feedback delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: Good cringe-comedy beat — Michael saying 'if you were nicer' to his own mother is both true and deeply uncomfortable, and the show plays it seriously enough to land.

jokes.text: I've been a horrible mother. / No, Mom. You're great. You've been a great mother. / Well, you know she's been a horrible mother, right? / You don't have to tell me.
jokes.setup: Lucille has a rare moment of self-awareness; Michael reflexively defends her
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately confirms to Michael that she IS a horrible mother; Michael agrees privately while having just said the opposite
jokes.explanation: Well-structured: Michael's public 'you're great' and private 'you don't have to tell me' in rapid succession is a clean joke about social performance. Gob's blunt confirmation is perfectly timed and delivered. The double irony (Lucille's moment of insight, Michael's hollow denial) works efficiently.

jokes.text: Um, is it okay if I do it for Mom and not you? / Yeah. / 'Cause I really like Mom. / We know.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to motivate Gob to attend the party; Gob parses his own motivation finely
jokes.punchline: Gob says he'd do it 'for Mom and not you,' then sincerely declares he really likes Mom — as if this is notable; Michael's 'we know' suggests Gob's mommy issues are an open secret
jokes.explanation: A good Gob beat — his insistence on the precise terms of his participation, and his sincere declaration of liking Mom, plays on his established mother fixation. 'We know' is the perfect Michael button.

jokes.text: I've already got a Lucille in my life. / I understand. That's healthy.
jokes.setup: Buster tells Lucille 2 he can't be with her
jokes.punchline: His reason is that he 'already has a Lucille' — his mother — and Lucille 2 calls this 'healthy,' when it is obviously the opposite
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's very best jokes. The implication that Buster can only manage one Lucille — and considers his MOTHER to be his primary romantic competitor — is deeply funny and deeply disturbing. Lucille 2's 'I understand. That's healthy.' — delivered presumably with complete sincerity — is the perfect button because it normalizes the most abnormal thing possible. Extremely high character integration: this joke is impossible without Buster's specific psychology. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Yes. I'm foolish and I'm funny and I'm needy. / Am I needy? / No. / Are you sure I'm not needy? 'Cause I feel needy sometimes.
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 has a moment of self-deprecating honesty; Buster assures her she's not needy
jokes.punchline: She immediately and repeatedly asks for reassurance that she's not needy — proving the point
jokes.explanation: Classic ironic self-undermining comedy: the character demonstrates the exact quality she's asking to be reassured about. The repetition ('Are you sure I'm not needy? 'Cause I feel needy sometimes') escalates the irony and is very funny. The structure is tight — denial of self-assessment immediately followed by evidence of that self-assessment.

jokes.text: You know, we're probably not that different, you and me. / You and I. Is it 'I' or is it 'me'? / Buster, it's us. / Yes. It is us. Us.
jokes.setup: Buster tries to make a heartfelt connection with Lucille 2
jokes.punchline: Lucille 2 corrects his grammar; he derails into a grammar debate; she redirects to 'us'; he repeats 'Us' with apparent profundity — the emotional moment is completely derailed by Buster's literal-mindedness
jokes.explanation: A beautifully Buster moment — he derails a romantic beat into a grammar discussion without any awareness that this is odd. Lucille 2's pivot to 'us' (meant romantically) being received by Buster as merely the grammatically correct pronoun is excellently layered. The final 'Yes. It is us. Us.' — where Buster seems to find this profound — completes the joke perfectly.

jokes.text: [9-minute mark: Buster and Lucille 2 scene continues into a long, unresolved awkward pause before scene cuts]
jokes.setup: Buster and Lucille 2 have reached an emotional peak with 'us'
jokes.punchline: The scene just... sits there, with both characters presumably staring at each other having solved nothing
jokes.explanation: The scene's comedic inertia after the grammar/pronoun exchange creates an awkward beat — the unresolved emotional situation is itself the joke. Less sharp than the preceding moments but functions as a coda.

jokes.text: Michael, once again, arrived at the restaurant. — Surprise! — And once again, no one showed up.
jokes.setup: Michael attempts a second surprise party for Lucille
jokes.punchline: The exact same outcome — 'once again, no one showed up' — makes it a running gag and escalates the futility
jokes.explanation: Effective running gag. The 'once again' framing mirrors the earlier attempt precisely, which is the joke — nothing has changed despite the episode's emotional content. The repetition is the point.

jokes.text: Get up. I told you. They don't support me. / I hate to tell you, Mom, but they don't support me either.
jokes.setup: Lucille is found moping; she complains her family doesn't support her
jokes.punchline: Michael, who organized the party FOR her while she moped, points out that they also don't support HIM — undermining her self-pity with his own
jokes.explanation: A solid beat that makes Michael's exhaustion with the family explicit while being funny. The reversal — he came to comfort her and ended up competing in a self-pity contest — is well-timed.

jokes.text: You did that for me? / I let you drive, didn't I?
jokes.setup: Lucille is touched that Michael advocated for her to the family
jokes.punchline: Michael's evidence of sacrifice: he let the world's worst driver drive
jokes.explanation: A callback that also plants the car accident — Michael giving Lucille the wheel is framed as a great sacrifice given everything we know about her driving. The line is economical and funny, and on rewatch it takes on dark significance as the setup for the accident.

jokes.text: It's an idiot on a scooter at night? It's gotta be Gob.
jokes.setup: Michael and Lucille spot someone on a scooter
jokes.punchline: Michael's immediate deduction that any idiot on a scooter at night must be Gob
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable Gob character joke. Michael's logical chain — idiot + scooter + nighttime = Gob — is perfectly Bluth sibling dynamics. The confidence of 'It's gotta be Gob' tells us everything about how the family views him.

jokes.text: Hey, big shot, too busy to go to your mother's party? / Mom, that is not Gob. Mom! Mom!
jokes.setup: Lucille wants to scare who she thinks is Gob on a scooter; Michael protests it's not him
jokes.punchline: Lucille drives into a stranger on a scooter — the accident happens because she acted on the wrong identification
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the entire driving setup — Lucille's vehicular violence is literalized. The escalation from jokey 'let's give him a scare' to actual accident is well-paced. On rewatch the full plot mechanics (Lucille deliberately hitting Michael with the champagne bottle) add an extra layer.

jokes.text: Yeah. I guess. How's my mom? She's so frail. / I'm fine. I'm fine. I was thrown clear.
jokes.setup: Michael has just been in a car accident; asks about his mother's welfare
jokes.punchline: He's asking about the woman who just crashed the car — and she's the one assuring HIM she's fine ('thrown clear')
jokes.explanation: Great ironic reversal: Michael's first concern post-accident is the frailty of the woman who caused it, and she's the one announcing she's fine. 'I was thrown clear' is also darkly funny — she was apparently not in the car when it crashed, suggesting speed/impact.

jokes.text: Your mother asked me to pump you full of... / Children's aspirin. / Okay.
jokes.setup: Michael is being treated; someone starts to reveal what Lucille ordered for his medication
jokes.punchline: The dramatic ellipsis resolves to 'Children's aspirin' — the most minimal, harmless possible medication
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'Children's aspirin' does real comic work — the buildup suggests something sinister or dramatic, the payoff is maximum bathos. Lucille's choice to give her adult son Children's aspirin is also perfectly in character.

jokes.text: 'Scoot'? Scooter. Was there a scooter involved?
jokes.setup: Michael has short-term memory loss; Lucille says 'scoot up' (meaning move over in bed)
jokes.punchline: Michael's word-association jumps to 'scooter' — he's on the verge of piecing together the accident from verbal cues
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed memory recovery bit — Michael's brain latches onto 'scoot' as a trigger word. The dramatic irony (we know what happened; he doesn't) makes this funnier. Lucille's panic at 'Oh, dear. I can't believe I did that' — immediately following — confirms she understands what's happening and is covering it up.

jokes.text: Oh, dear. I can't believe I did that. / I am so sorry. I'll get you some air. / I hardly felt it. In fact, actually, I didn't feel it.
jokes.setup: Michael is close to figuring out the accident; Lucille panics and redirects
jokes.punchline: Michael, who was clearly hit on the head, says 'I didn't feel it' — the head injury is making him easy to manipulate; Lucille uses the distraction perfectly
jokes.explanation: Part of the sustained manipulation sequence — works as part of the episode's larger plot comedy rather than as a standalone joke.

jokes.text: Buster, you remember when we were kissing last night? It was a wild, wild ride.
jokes.setup: Buster has just woken up/arrived; Lucille 2 references their newfound romantic connection
jokes.punchline: She describes kissing Buster as 'a wild, wild ride' — an absurdly grandiose description for what we can only imagine was extremely awkward
jokes.explanation: The image of Buster kissing being described as a 'wild, wild ride' is inherently funny. Works on character-based absurdism.

jokes.text: Is this something we can do? Is this something society will allow?
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 is contemplating her romance with Buster, who is significantly younger
jokes.punchline: She asks if 'society will allow' this — echoing the cousin subplot and the show's pattern of characters seeking external permission for transgressive relationships
jokes.explanation: Rhymes thematically with the George Michael/Maeby storyline — both relationships ask what society will allow. The callback is probably intentional: the episode is building a theme around transgressive love and external judgment. 'Something society will allow' is a wonderfully formal way to discuss a May-December romance.

jokes.text: My God, for the first time in years I felt like I was standing on solid ground. You know, my panic attacks have decreased. I feel confident. I'm feeling proud even.
jokes.setup: Buster describes how his relationship with Lucille 2 has helped his anxiety
jokes.punchline: The irony: he's replaced one controlling older woman (Mom) with another (Lucille 2) and calls this psychological growth
jokes.explanation: Good character irony — Buster's self-reported growth is based entirely on finding a Lucille substitute. The joke is implicit but clear.

jokes.text: You know, I should call George Michael. I'm worried about my son. / I'm worried about my son. George Michael will be fine with his aunt and uncle. / I want you resting, not using the phone. Let me take care of you.
jokes.setup: Michael, head-injured and confused, wants to call his son
jokes.punchline: Lucille — who caused the accident — positions herself as Michael's caretaker and denies him the phone, which conveniently prevents him from talking to anyone who might tell him the truth
jokes.explanation: On rewatch this is a key manipulation beat — Lucille as caring mother is a complete act designed to keep Michael isolated and confused. The dark comedy of the abuser playing nurse is characteristically AD.

jokes.text: Scare. Did somebody say, 'Let's give him a sc...' What is the matter with me? Clumsy Clara.
jokes.setup: Michael says something that triggers Lucille's memory of the accident
jokes.punchline: She catches herself mid-revelation, pivots to self-deprecating 'Clumsy Clara' — a completely fabricated persona she's never shown before — to cover
jokes.explanation: A great manipulation-catch moment. 'Clumsy Clara' is invented on the spot — Lucille spontaneously creates a self-deprecating persona she's never used before (she is NOT self-deprecating) to cover her near-confession. The absurdity of 'Clumsy Clara' coming from Lucille Bluth is very funny and reveals her quick-thinking villainy.

jokes.text: Totally cool. Didn't feel a thing. You know, Mom, I'm crazy about this aspirin. I can't believe we give it to children.
jokes.setup: Michael is high on Children's aspirin following his head injury
jokes.punchline: He raves about the aspirin as if it's a powerful drug — which it isn't — while claiming to feel nothing from a serious head injury
jokes.explanation: Funny callback to the Children's aspirin joke — Michael is apparently so out of it that baby aspirin feels profound. 'I can't believe we give it to children' is quotable and perfectly landed. The combination of his braggadocio about feeling 'totally cool' while clearly being brain-addled is good physical-state comedy.

jokes.text: Michael? George Michael. / You've gotta go. / Wait. Uncle Gob, is this a bad time? / No. Are you kidding? So, what's up?
jokes.setup: George Michael arrives at the yacht to find Gob with a woman; Gob is clearly caught
jokes.punchline: Gob's pivot from 'You've gotta go' (to the woman) to 'No. Are you kidding?' (to George Michael) — he clears the woman but overcompensates with his nephew
jokes.explanation: Standard caught-with-someone scene, but Gob's enthusiastic pivot to 'Are you kidding? So, what's up?' is funny in its eagerness to seem normal.

jokes.text: I need you to make some fake I.D.'s for me and Maeby. / Like a passport? / Yeah, yeah. That'd be great. Oh, and, uh, preferably French. I like the way they think.
jokes.setup: George Michael asks Gob for fake IDs to see an adult movie
jokes.punchline: George Michael escalates 'fake ID' to 'passport' and then specifies 'French' because he admires French thinking — a completely non sequitur rationale for a fake identity document
jokes.explanation: George Michael's request for a French passport because he 'likes the way they think' is both deeply in character (bookish, romanticized notion of French intellectualism) and completely absurd as a practical consideration for sneaking into a movie. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Look, I don't want you thinking that your uncle is some sleazy character from the docks. / Good morning. / How are you?
jokes.setup: Gob assures George Michael he's not a sleazy dockside character; a woman walks by and he greets her warmly (clearly she's just leaving the yacht)
jokes.punchline: The woman's appearance immediately after his denial proves he is exactly a sleazy character from the docks
jokes.explanation: Perfect visual irony — Gob's declaration is immediately falsified by evidence. The timing (denial followed in the same breath by the contradiction) is immaculate. This is the joke that structural comedy dreams of. The 'she's an Olympian... hopeful' followup only deepens it.

jokes.text: She's an Olympian... hopeful.
jokes.setup: Gob has just been caught with a woman despite claiming he's not sleazy
jokes.punchline: He offers 'Olympian... hopeful' as her credential — the pause before 'hopeful' indicates he's softening the status in real time, and 'hopeful' is an almost meaningless credential
jokes.explanation: A miniature masterpiece of economy. The pause implied by the ellipsis — Gob downgrading 'Olympian' to 'Olympian hopeful' in real time — is the entire joke. He's establishing her respectability while simultaneously revealing he's just met her. 'Swimming and some coaching' in the next line extends the deflation. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: But we're gonna have to make this quick. Your dad wants me out of here by noon. / I wouldn't worry about him. He's staying at Gangy's. / Yeah, he got in a car accident on the way back from her birthday party. / Man, I told him not to let her drive. / No. It was him driving.
jokes.setup: Gob expresses urgency about Michael's deadline; George Michael reassures him Michael can't interfere
jokes.punchline: Gob assumes Lucille was driving (she usually is — the dangerous one); George Michael corrects him — Michael was driving — a reversal from what we know
jokes.explanation: Good irony — Gob's assumption that Lucille drove (totally reasonable given her record) is wrong in the official version of events. On rewatch we know the official version is itself a lie, adding another layer.

jokes.text: So you're telling me that he can't go anywhere and he's incommunicado? / That's when Gob decided to take the yacht to South America for the summer.
jokes.setup: Gob hears Michael is bedridden and isolated
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Gob immediately pivots to exploiting his brother's incapacitation to flee to South America
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gob logic — the instant Michael is out of commission, Gob's first thought is to exploit it for maximum personal benefit. 'For the summer' implies he's already planned the whole thing. The narrator's deadpan delivery of this revelation is excellent.

jokes.text: I gotta alert this boat's crew. They'll be devastated.
jokes.setup: Gob announces his South America plan to George Michael
jokes.punchline: His immediate concern is telling the yacht crew — whom he assumes are eagerly awaiting his leadership — that they're going to South America. The assumption that they'll be 'devastated' to learn this good news is absurd.
jokes.explanation: Gob's self-importance — assuming his crew will be devastated to hear they're going to South America (presumably a great trip) — is perfectly in character. The word 'devastated' doing triple work (ironic, character-revealing, plot-functional) is economical.

jokes.text: Dr. Miller? / Yeah. / I just jumped through a plate glass window on a date.
jokes.setup: Michael is resting; Buster arrives and calls a doctor
jokes.punchline: Dr. Miller's casual revelation that he just jumped through plate glass 'on a date' — a deeply unusual date activity delivered with complete nonchalance
jokes.explanation: A beautifully delivered non sequitur. 'I just jumped through a plate glass window on a date' has perfect economy — the juxtaposition of violent/dangerous action with the casual social context 'on a date' is very funny. The deadpan delivery implied makes it better.

jokes.text: Oh. Well, women love to laugh. / It was worth it to be away from Mom's controlling grasp.
jokes.setup: Dr. Miller explains jumping through glass as a dating tactic
jokes.punchline: Buster's response: it was worth it to be 'away from Mom's controlling grasp' — revealing this extreme act was primarily motivated by escaping his mother, not romance
jokes.explanation: Good Buster moment — his escape from maternal control is so imperative that physical injury is considered a bargain. The phrase 'Mom's controlling grasp' is vivid and character-perfect.

jokes.text: Buster, what's going on? What happened to your head? / Nothing. Gob was just teaching me how to hit it with a hammer.
jokes.setup: Michael notices Buster's head injury and asks what happened
jokes.punchline: Buster's explanation — Gob was 'teaching him' to hit his own head with a hammer — is both absurd and completely plausible given what we know about Gob and Buster
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best standalone jokes. The image of Gob 'teaching' Buster to hit himself with a hammer is simultaneously absurd and 100% consistent with both characters. It's the kind of thing you can imagine actually happening. 'Just teaching me how to hit it with a hammer' — delivered by Buster as if this is a perfectly normal explanation — has perfect economy and near-perfect character integration. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Gob was there. Gob was on his scooter. I think I wanted to hit Gob. Yes. That is exactly what happened. You wanted to give Gob a scare.
jokes.setup: Michael is piecing together the accident memory; Lucille 'helps' him
jokes.punchline: Lucille feeds Michael a false memory in real time — her 'yes, that is EXACTLY what happened' confirms she's actively rewriting the event while he's confused and compliant
jokes.explanation: Exceptionally dark comedy. Lucille is literally implanting false memories in her injured son, and the show plays it almost tenderly — she sounds helpful and confirming. The gap between her apparent nurturing and her actual villainy is the joke. High rewatch value because you see the manipulation clearly on second viewing.

jokes.text: That's it. I don't want... You can't... I don't... Please, you gotta stop coming here.
jokes.setup: Lindsay returns to prison for another visit with her father
jokes.punchline: George Sr. is visibly falling apart — he can't complete a sentence — the implication being that Lindsay's prison visits are so disruptive (presumably due to inmate reactions) that he's desperate for her to stop
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the prison subplot — the joke has flipped: we assumed no one would react to Lindsay, but George Sr.'s inability to finish sentences suggests her visits ARE causing pandemonium (at his expense). The gap between Lindsay's expectation and reality has reversed, adding complexity.

jokes.text: Well, what about me? This is the third time I've come here. I haven't even been remotely harassed.
jokes.setup: George Sr. begs Lindsay to stop coming because it's too disruptive/expensive for him
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's counter-complaint is that she HASN'T been harassed — her primary grievance is not being objectified enough
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's funniest and most densely layered jokes. Lindsay's complaint that she hasn't been 'remotely harassed' — expressed as a GRIEVANCE — perfectly caps her character arc in this subplot. It inverts our expectations, inverts Lindsay's expectations, and reveals the depth of her vanity in a single devastating line. The delivery makes it funnier: she's genuinely aggrieved. This is among the episode's best.

jokes.text: Have I lost it? Did I ever even have it?
jokes.setup: Lindsay is devastated by prison inmates' indifference to her
jokes.punchline: She wonders if she ever had the attractiveness she's built her identity around — played for pathos but the show frames this as comedically pathetic
jokes.explanation: A decent cringe-comedy beat — Lindsay's existential crisis about her looks is both funny and slightly sad, which is the Lindsay sweet spot.

jokes.text: I'm paying thousands of dollars in Krugerrands. / What? / Gold. Krugerrands. / Your mother snuck 'em in here stuffed in energy bar wrappers to keep me from getting strangled in the shower or worse. / Stabbed? / In a way.
jokes.setup: George Sr. reveals the protein/energy bars were actually gold coins
jokes.punchline: He's been paying prison protection money in Krugerrands smuggled by Lucille — 'or worse' / 'Stabbed?' / 'In a way' implies the 'worse' is sexual, adding dark humor
jokes.explanation: Major payoff to the protein bar/gold bar setup from the episode's opening. The Krugerrand reveal is excellently structured: the comedy of Lucille smuggling gold in energy bar wrappers, the prison-economy detail, and the escalating 'stabbed? / in a way' dark joke about prison sexual assault (handled with just enough obliqueness). Excellent callback architecture.

jokes.text: But you keep coming back here, honey, and I'm going broke.
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains he's paying off prisoners to protect Lindsay from harassment
jokes.punchline: The punchline: he's going BROKE protecting her from the harassing that she actively WANTS to experience
jokes.explanation: The episode's most perfectly ironic moment of the Lindsay subplot: Dad is spending his gold reserves to prevent what Lindsay is furious he's preventing. Neither knows what the other wants, and the result is comedic tragedy at its finest.

jokes.text: That's all I ever wanted from you, Daddy. For you to spend money on me.
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains he's going broke paying for her prison protection; Lindsay is touched
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's takeaway from 'Dad is spending money protecting me' is that this is the fatherly love she always craved — she's just happy about the spending, not the protection
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest jokes and most quotable lines. Lindsay's emotional revelation — that all she ever wanted was for Dad to spend money on her — perfectly encapsulates her character in one line. It's simultaneously sad (she mistakes expenditure for love) and hilariously mercenary. The delivery lands as genuine emotion from her, which makes it funnier. Economy is near-perfect: nine words that say everything about Lindsay Bluth.

jokes.text: No touching! [guard shouts as Lindsay and George Sr. have an emotional moment]
jokes.setup: Lindsay and George Sr. are having a touching emotional reconciliation moment
jokes.punchline: The 'No touching!' guard interruption — the show's recurring prison visit gag — deflates the sentiment perfectly
jokes.explanation: The season's recurring gag — and it works perfectly here as a deflation device after Lindsay's moving line. The timing earns it a higher impact than craft score because the show uses it as punctuation. Low originality because it's a pre-established running gag.

jokes.text: Gob, I want you to move back onto the yacht. / The yacht? What makes you think I'd want to now?
jokes.setup: Michael, concussed, generously offers Gob the yacht
jokes.punchline: Gob — who has just been planning to take the yacht to South America — performs surprise that he'd 'want' the yacht, as if it's not his entire plan
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony — we know Gob desperately wants the yacht; his feigned indifference is transparent to the audience even as it works on concussed Michael. Character-integrated and efficient.

jokes.text: Wow. How hard did you hit that steering wheel? / I actually hit the back of my head.
jokes.setup: Michael says 'it's just money' — completely out of character; Gob is alarmed
jokes.punchline: Michael corrects Gob's assumption — he didn't hit the steering wheel (front of head) but the BACK of his head, which is the actual clue to the cover-up
jokes.explanation: A plot-mechanism joke that's also genuinely funny. The 'back of the head' detail is the clue that unravels the conspiracy, planted here with Gob's growing suspicion. The comedy comes from Michael not realizing what he's revealing while Gob pieces it together.

jokes.text: Turn this skiff around. / We haven't even left the dock. / But 'skiff' is appropriate, right?
jokes.setup: Gob orders the boat to turn around dramatically
jokes.punchline: The crew member points out they haven't moved; Gob asks if 'skiff' (a small flat-bottomed boat) is the correct nautical term — he's more concerned with his vocabulary than the situation
jokes.explanation: Excellent Gob joke. His dramatic order is deflated by the revelation they haven't moved — but rather than responding to this information, Gob redirects to asking if his word choice was nautically correct. This is perfectly Gob: vain, performative, prioritizing appearance over substance. Highly quotable and character-specific.

jokes.text: There's no way you were trying to hurt me. You don't hate me. Mom hates me. You kind of like me.
jokes.setup: Michael confesses he might have wanted to hurt Gob
jokes.punchline: Gob's analysis of the sibling-maternal dynamic — setting 'hate' as the standard and 'kind of like' as the best Michael achieves — reveals how low the bar is in this family
jokes.explanation: Beautifully calibrated family dysfunction joke. The gradation — Mom HATES, Michael KIND OF LIKES — as Gob's framework for evaluating affection tells us everything about the Bluth emotional ecosystem. 'You kind of like me' as the ceiling of brotherly love is both funny and touching. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Yeah. I kind of like you. / Yeah.
jokes.setup: Gob declares Michael 'kind of likes' him
jokes.punchline: Michael confirms it with complete sincerity — 'yeah, I kind of like you' — and both brothers accept this as sufficient
jokes.explanation: The button to joke #79, and it's perfect. Michael's confirmation — 'yeah, I kind of like you' — and both brothers nodding with satisfaction is the funniest and most poignant thing in the episode. 'Kind of like' is the warmest these people get, and they're both fine with it. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Michael, I need your help. / Coming. / Ow! I got hit back there. / Right! On the back of the head, which means that you couldn't have hit the steering wheel.
jokes.setup: The sweet sibling moment is interrupted by Michael bumping his head again (presumably on the door frame)
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately uses the new head-bump as forensic evidence — 'on the back of the head!' — connecting the dots of the cover-up in real time
jokes.explanation: Good physical-to-plot comedy: Michael literally walks into evidence that helps Gob crack the case. The fact that a fresh head-bump becomes forensic data is the right kind of escalating absurdity for this show.

jokes.text: Mom must have clubbed you with a champagne bottle or something so she could drive. / Gob, how'd you figure it out? / It's funny you should ask that... / No. He didn't figure anything out.
jokes.setup: Gob presents his theory confidently; Michael asks how he figured it out
jokes.punchline: The narrator interrupts to deflate Gob's detective moment — 'He didn't figure anything out.' The real answer is Michael's rock.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional narrator-as-deflator moment. Gob is poised to take credit for detective work; the narrator brutally steps in mid-sentence to deny it. This is AD's narrator at his most functionally comedic — not just describing action but actively undermining a character's self-image. The structure (Gob's build + narrator's interruption) is flawlessly timed, and the actual explanation (the rock) is a perfect callback to Joke #28. Among the episode's best.

jokes.text: I probably got hit on the back of the head because of Buster's rock. Hey, big shot! Which was behind the passenger seat. Which means that she picked you up and dragged you over to the driver's seat.
jokes.setup: The brothers piece together the accident; Michael realizes the rock (established earlier) explains the injury
jokes.punchline: The full conspiracy unfolds: Lucille used Buster's rock as a weapon, then physically moved Michael's body — which requires her to be strong, not frail
jokes.explanation: The payoff of multiple planted details — the rock, the 'frail' act, the protein bar strength. The reconstruction is satisfying both as plot and comedy: each piece snaps into place from earlier in the episode. 'Hey, big shot!' is used here as a narrator callback (Lucille's own words) — brilliant. The earned_vs_cheap score is high because every element was legitimately planted.

jokes.text: Mom can't pick me up. She could barely pick up that bag of protein bars. / Guess what? They weren't protein bars. They were gold Krugerrands.
jokes.setup: Michael uses 'frail Lucille' logic to discount the theory
jokes.punchline: Gob reveals the protein bars were gold coins — so the bag was even HEAVIER than protein bars, proving Lucille has been faking weakness
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke and one of the finest examples of AD's structural comedy. The protein bars (established early as a throwaway) are revealed to be gold coins (established in the prison subplot), and this revelation SIMULTANEOUSLY proves Lucille's strength (the bag was heavy with gold, not light protein bars), debunks the frailty act, and closes multiple plot threads. This is the exact moment where all the episode's planted jokes detonate simultaneously. Structure score is near-perfect because multiple callbacks converge in a single line. This is what rewards rewatching — on first view it's very funny; on rewatch you see the architecture.

jokes.text: And Dad finally paid attention to my intellect.
jokes.setup: Gob has just deduced (partially) that the protein bars were Krugerrands
jokes.punchline: Gob frames this as his father finally acknowledging his 'intellect' — when he's actually just the last person to learn information everyone else now knows
jokes.explanation: Good character beat — Gob interprets learning about his own family's conspiracy as intellectual achievement worthy of his father's recognition. The gap between his self-perception and reality is pure Gob.

jokes.text: I was halfway to South America, but I couldn't let you get away with it, because we're brothers, Mom, and we kind of like each other.
jokes.setup: Gob confronts Lucille about the setup
jokes.punchline: 'We kind of like each other' — the sibling bond expressed in the episode's established vocabulary of minimal affection — applied here to justify Gob turning the yacht around
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to the 'kind of like each other' exchange — now used by Gob as a sincere declaration of brotherhood. The understatement ('kind of like each other') being the emotional climax of the episode is exactly AD's tone. Very quotable and emotionally landed without being sentimental.

jokes.text: You were going to South America? / I don't think so.
jokes.setup: Gob has just heroically declared he turned around for his brother; Lucille focuses on the South America part
jokes.punchline: Michael's flat 'I don't think so' — he's not letting Gob off the hook for the South America plan just because Gob did the right thing
jokes.explanation: A well-timed button — Michael refuses to let the emotional resolution paper over Gob's actual plans. 'I don't think so' is perfectly deadpan and grounds the scene.

jokes.text: I don't even remember you ever doing that before. / I just want my children to love me. / Stop lying. Stop manipulating. Just be nicer.
jokes.setup: Michael confronts Lucille about her manipulation; she pivots to vulnerability
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'Stop lying. Stop manipulating. Just be nicer.' — a parental injunction delivered to his own mother, as if she's the child
jokes.explanation: The role-reversal comedy of Michael parenting his mother is played straight, which makes it funnier. 'Just be nicer' as the reductive solution to a lifetime of manipulation is perfect.

jokes.text: I'm a horrible mother. / No, no, Mom. You're great. / That's crazy talk. / You're a fantastic mother. / I can't believe what I'm hearing, Mother. / Oh, we do. We really love you.
jokes.setup: Lucille again declares herself a horrible mother (an accurate statement)
jokes.punchline: Both brothers rush to deny it — even though moments before they both agreed she was a horrible mother — and Gob escalates to 'fantastic'
jokes.explanation: A callback to the earlier private agreement — the irony of both brothers immediately capitulating to Lucille's fishing for compliments after literally agreeing she's horrible is well-constructed. The escalation from Michael's 'you're great' to Gob's 'fantastic' is good.

jokes.text: George Michael and Maeby sneak back into Les Cousins Dangereux. / Excuse me. You're too young to be holding that ticket. / Let's go. / I can't go to jail. / Let's just watch cartoons.
jokes.setup: The stinger shows George Michael and Maeby's second attempt to see the forbidden cousin film
jokes.punchline: They're caught again — and 'I can't go to jail' suggests they may have tried something more elaborate (like the French passports) that went further wrong
jokes.explanation: Good 'On the Next' stinger that pays off both the film subplot and the fake ID plan. 'I can't go to jail' implies the passport scheme escalated beyond the movie theater. 'Let's just watch cartoons' is a perfect deflating button.

jokes.text: Sir, we will not be starting with Les Cousins... until you leave. / Excuse me, but I thought my A.A.R.P. card was good for one and a guest. / I knew society would never accept this. And they never will.
jokes.setup: The theater staff won't start the film; an elderly man waves his AARP card; George Michael despairs
jokes.punchline: George Michael interprets the theater's refusal — which is about the AARP card situation — as society's verdict on his and Maeby's relationship
jokes.explanation: A superb triple-callback closer. The AARP situation has nothing to do with them; George Michael experiences it as cosmic rejection of his love. 'I knew society would never accept this' — echoing Lucille 2's 'Is this something society will allow?' and the narrator's 'the world thought was wrong' — ties the episode's transgressive-romance theme together in one absurd misunderstanding. Excellent structure.

jokes.text: Michael's insurance takes another hit. / Ow! / I was definitely driving that time.
jokes.setup: The second stinger — 'Michael's insurance takes another hit'
jokes.punchline: Michael is in another accident; he confirms 'I was definitely driving that time' — retroactively distinguishing this from the first accident where he wasn't driving
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode closer. 'I was definitely driving that time' has enormous callback value — it retroactively confirms the episode's central mystery (the first time he WASN'T driving, even though he thought he was) while being hilarious as a standalone. The economy is near-perfect. A great button on the entire episode's comedic architecture.

tmdb_overview: Michael attempts to fire the family's incompetent, but likeable attorney. George Michael impresses Maeby with a muscle suit for a "Living Classics" pagent. Tobias has a psychological affliction where he is unable to take off his clothes.
insight: Bluth family dysfunction peaks at 2.14 jokes-per-minute despite middling 78 score.
